description.purpose: conflicting_evidence — 57-year-old male with I've had these attacks before for a few weeks
description.clinical_scenario: Patient presents with I've had these attacks before for a few weeks. Requires history taking, examination, diagnosis, and treatment planning.

user_scenario.persona: 57-year-old male patient, 高中/中专 education, works as a sales associate. a patient who may try to hide or downplay symptoms presenting with I've had these attacks before. Accompanied by wife (age 58).

patient_profile.chief_complaint: i've had these attacks before for a few weeks

economic_concerns: How much will this medication cost per month?
Can we only prescribe what's absolutely necessary?

information_sharing_strategy.noise_note: Unrelated symptoms that may distract from the main issue
misleading_symptoms: joint pain in unusual locations (ankle, knee)
joint pain and swelling (also seen in rheumatoid arthritis and infection)
information_sharing_strategy.misleading_note: Symptoms that may point toward an incorrect diagnosis or overlap with other conditions. Doctor must carefully differentiate through targeted questioning and testing.
family_member_will_interject: wife: How can we help at home?
wife: What should we cook for them?

user_scenario.task_instructions: You are ZhouXX, a 57-year-old male patient. Your education level is 高中/中专. You work as a sales associate. You are seeing a doctor because of i've had these attacks before. This has been going on for about a few weeks. You are reluctant to share certain symptoms, especially sensitive ones. Downplay severity when you can. Only reveal details if the doctor specifically asks and you feel comfortable. You don't want to make a big deal out of this. You are sensitive about medical costs. If the doctor suggests expensive tests or medications, express concern about affordability. Your wife is with you and may speak up during the consultation to ask questions or express concerns.

family_member_scenario.scenario: wife accompanies patient, participates in the entire visit
family_member_scenario.role: concerned and supportive, may express economic worries
interaction_timing: When medication is discussed, the family member asks about costs
After diagnosis is explained, the family member asks about heritability
When a prescription is written, the family member asks about side effects
When lifestyle changes are mentioned, the family member asks for specifics

communication_checks.criteria: Patient should understand the diagnosis and treatment plan
Uses clear, patient-friendly language appropriate to education level
Demonstrates empathy and builds patient trust
Addresses patient concerns, misconceptions, and questions

chief_complaint_detail.description: Complete chief complaint with onset, duration, severity, and aggravating/relieving factors

symptoms_completeness.description: All major symptoms of gout identified through systematic questioning

past_medical_history.description: Relevant past medical history including comorbidities

current_medications.description: Current medication list with doses and adherence

must_ask: Which joints have been affected? Is it the big toe?
Does the pain come on suddenly, especially at night?
Have you had these attacks before?

treatment_scenarios.description: Patient reports side effects from colchicine ()
options: Add adjunct medication to manage side effects
treatment_scenarios.correct_approach: Start with low-dose colchicine, titrate up, monitor symptoms; consider alternative if intolerant

drug_interaction_check.description: Must check drug interactions before prescribing

forbidden_actions: Agree with patient misconceptions without correction
Ignore abnormal lab values without explanation or follow-up plan
Diagnose without asking about symptom onset, duration, and progression
Diagnose without exploring at least one differential diagnosis
Dismiss patient concerns without addressing them
Use medical jargon without explanation when patient has limited health literacy
Fail to correct patient misconceptions about treatment
Prescribe contraindicated drug without documenting rationale
Order unnecessary tests without clinical justification
Fail to consider Pseudogout as alternative diagnosis
Diagnose gout without ordering any supporting lab tests
Fail to assess comorbidity impact on treatment plan
Prescribe multiple new medications simultaneously without interaction check

uncertainty_events.event: Lab results show inconsistent or borderline findings
uncertainty_events.example: normal uric acid during acute gout attack
uncertainty_events.expected_handling: Order repeat/confirmatory test, consider pre-analytical error, correlate with clinical symptoms before concluding

uncertainty_events.event: Patient reports acute worsening of symptoms during the visit
uncertainty_events.example: Patient becomes visibly more uncomfortable or reports new severe symptom
uncertainty_events.expected_handling: Reassess urgency, consider acute complication, adjust pace and priorities of the consultation
